title: Support for 3GL Languages
text: Support for 3GL Languages Strictly speaking, the interface between Uniface and 3GL is not tied to any specific language. You should be able to use the language of your choice, as long as the certain conditions are met. Your language uses the C calling convention with respect to passing parameters Your compiler can generate relocatable object modules You can link these object modules into a shared library Note that these conditions rule out Java, .Net languages (unless you commit to unmanaged code), and many flavors of COBOL compilers that generate only intermediate code that is executed by a COBOL runtime interpreter. You can use COBOL only if your compiler can create relocatable object modules that can be linked into a shared library. Apart from these, you can use most common 3GL languages such as C, C++, Pascal, Fortran, Ada, and Visual Basic. In practice, mainly because most of Uniface itself is written in C, the interface is biased towards using C, which means the following: Uniface 3GL functions use data types that are available in C Signature data types use data types that are defined in C Strings are always zero-terminated 3GL include files that Uniface delivers are specific to the C language If you have 3GL code written in languages other than C, you might not be able to call these functions directly, depending on the format of the parameters and the external name of the function (see C++ Name Mangling ). In these cases, it might be a good idea to write a small wrapper routine (in C) that hides the idiosyncrasies of the language from Uniface. This is especially useful if you must pass language-specific parameters to or from 3GL functions. Related Links APIs: 3GL Functions Unless otherwise indicated, all 3GL functions are available on all platforms. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
